_meta.authoringNotes: Tyler KL. Handb Clin Neurol 2014 enterovirus neurology. WHO EV-A71 fact sheet 2023.rotterdam J Neurovirol 2022 enterovirus D68. Bahl AM et al. EV-D68 neurology 2023. Gaunt ER et al. J Infect Dis 2009 echovirus epidemiology.

h1Title: Enteroviral Encephalitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis and Treatment
heroOverview: Enteroviral encephalitis is inflammation of the brain parenchyma caused by enteroviruses — a genus of non-enveloped, positive-sense single-stranded RNA viruses within the Picornaviridae family that includes poliovirus, coxsackievirus A and B, echovirus, enterovirus D68 (EV-D68), and enterovirus A71 (EV-A71). Enteroviruses are among the most common human pathogens globally, causing an estimated 1 billion infections annually — most as asymptomatic or mild febrile illness, but a small proportion invade the CNS to cause meningitis, encephalitis, or acute flaccid myelitis (AFM). EV-A71 has caused large outbreaks of hand-foot-and-mouth disease with fatal neurological complications in the Asia-Pacific region (1997-2008 Taiwan, 2012-2018 China). EV-D68 emerged as a neurological pathogen causing AFM outbreaks in 2014, 2016, 2018, and 2022 in the US and internationally. Encephalitis presents with fever, altered mental status, seizures, and focal neurological signs depending on the brain region involved. CSF typically shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose and mildly elevated protein. Diagnosis is by PCR of CSF, throat swab, stool, or blood. Treatment is primarily supportive — no specific antiviral therapy exists for non-polio enteroviruses. Prevention focuses on hand hygiene, infection control, and EV-A71 vaccine development (vacccines available in China since 2016).

keyStats.prevalence: Enteroviruses cause ~1 billion infections annually; CNS invasion in 0.1-0.5% of cases; encephalitis accounts for a fraction of enteroviral CNS disease
keyStats.demographics: Children <5 years bear the highest burden of enteroviral infections and encephalitis; most EV-D68 neurological cases in children; EV-A71 encephalitis primarily in children <5 years
keyStats.avgAge: Peak enteroviral encephalitis incidence <5 years; EV-D68 AFM median age 5-6 years
keyStats.globalCases: Enteroviral CNS disease (meningitis + encephalitis) estimated at 300,000-500,000 cases annually globally; encephalitis represents ~10-20% of enteroviral CNS infections

definition: Enteroviral encephalitis (ICD-10: A85.0) is viral inflammation of the brain parenchyma caused by enteroviruses. Enteroviruses are small (30nm) non-enveloped RNA viruses with a genome of ~7,500 nucleotides encoding a single polyprotein that is cleaved into structural (capsid proteins VP1-VP4) and non-structural proteins. Enteroviruses infect via the fecal-oral route (feco-oral transmission) for most types, or respiratory droplets for EV-D68 and EV-70/71. Primary replication occurs in the oropharyngeal and intestinal mucosa; viremia follows, and in a small minority, the virus crosses the blood-brain barrier to infect neural tissue. Different enteroviruses have tropism for specific CNS cells: EV-A71 strongly infects motor neurons (causing AFM and brainstem encephalitis), while echoviruses and coxsackie B tend to cause meningitis and meningoencephalitis. EV-D68 has tropism for respiratory epithelium but also binds to neural receptors (PSA receptor) causing motor neuron infection and AFM. CSF findings: lymphocytic pleocytosis (typically 50-1,000 cells/µL), normal glucose, and protein elevation (usually <150 mg/dL). MRI brain may show T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the brainstem, deep gray nuclei, or cortical areas depending on virus. Infectious disease specialists and neurologists manage enteroviral encephalitis.

typesClassification.description: The most common cause of viral encephalitis in many settings. Echoviruses (especially types 4, 6, 9, 11, 13, 18, 30) and coxsackie B viruses (types 1-5) cause seasonal summer-fall outbreaks of aseptic meningitis with occasional encephalitis. Generally favorable prognosis.

Caused by enterovirus A71. In Asia-Pacific outbreaks, EV-A71 caused severe neurological disease including brainstem encephalitis and cardiorespiratory failure. Characteristic hand-foot-mouth disease with neurological involvement. Case fatality 5-10% in severe cases; survivors may have neurological sequelae.

Enterovirus D68, primarily a respiratory virus, causes sudden-onset flaccid weakness resembling poliomyelitis in children during respiratory virus seasons. MRI shows longitudinal spinal cord gray matter lesions. Most severely affected children have residual permanent weakness. No specific treatment exists.

Wild poliovirus and vaccine-derived poliovirus (VDPV) cause paralytic poliomyelitis — a form of enteroviral 'encephalitis' with selective motor neuron destruction in the anterior horn of the spinal cord. Near-eradication globally; remaining cases in Afghanistan and Pakistan. Prevented by IPV/OPV vaccination.

primarySymptoms: Fever — often high (38.5-40°C), may be biphasic with initial respiratory/febrile phase followed by CNS phase
Altered mental status — confusion, agitation, lethargy, or decreased consciousness depending on brain region involved
Seizures — focal or generalized, present in 30-50% of enteroviral encephalitis; may be the presenting symptom
Neck stiffness (meningismus) — accompanies encephalitis when meninges are also involved (meningoencephalitis)
Focal neurological deficits — depending on brain region: ataxia (cerebellum), cranial nerve palsy (brainstem), hemiparesis (cerebral cortex), visual field defects
Myoclonus (involuntary muscle jerks) — in brainstem or basal ganglia involvement
Respiratory symptoms — concurrent respiratory illness is common in EV-D68 infections
Skin rash — hand-foot-mouth rash with oral vesicles in EV-A71 and coxsackie A infections
Acute flaccid limb weakness — in EV-D68 AFM or poliomyelitis
earlyWarningSigns: Acute flaccid limb weakness in a child with recent respiratory illness — suspect EV-D68 AFM
Altered mental status with seizures in summer-fall season — enterovirus season; may be confused with HSV encephalitis
Brainstem signs (facial weakness, dysphagia, ataxia, irregular breathing) with hand-foot-mouth history — EV-A71 brainstem encephalitis
Rapid progression from fever to deep coma within 24-48 hours — severe encephalitis requiring ICU
New focal neurological deficits in a child with viral prodrome — enteroviral encephalitis vs stroke
emergencySigns: Acute respiratory failure — EV-A71 brainstem encephalitis can cause cardiorespiratory arrest through brainstem dysfunction
Refractory seizures and status epilepticus — enteroviral encephalitis can cause super-refractory status epilepticus
Rapidly progressive flaccid paralysis — EV-D68 AFM or poliomyelitis requires urgent MRI spine and neurology
Increased intracranial pressure (decerebrate posturing, absent pupillary reflexes) — severe brain edema from encephalitis
Encephalopathy with hemodynamic instability — severe enteroviral encephalitis with cytokine storm

causes.description: Enteroviruses replicate in the mucosa, enter the bloodstream (viremia), and cross the blood-brain barrier to infect neural tissue. Viral tropism for CNS cells and host immune response determine disease severity.

causes.cause: Direct viral cytopathic effect in brain parenchyma
causes.description: Enteroviruses replicate within neural cells (neurons, glia), causing direct cell death and inflammation. The virus triggers local cytokine production (IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-α) that contributes to edema and clinical symptoms.
In some cases, the host's own immune response (autoantibodies, T-cell mediated cytotoxicity) contributes to neural damage beyond direct viral effect — similar to post-infectious encephalitis.

causes.cause: EV-D68 binding to neural PSA receptor
causes.description: EV-D68 uses polysialic acid (PSA) receptors on neural cells to enter motor neurons, explaining its tropism for the anterior horn cell and causing AFM rather than diffuse encephalitis.

riskFactors.description: Children have the highest incidence of enteroviral infections and CNS invasion due to lack of prior immunity and immature blood-brain barrier.
Enteroviruses peak in summer and early fall in temperate climates (July-September in northern hemisphere), coinciding with the enteroviral encephalitis season.
Patients with agammaglobulinemia or other B-cell deficiencies have chronic enteroviral CNS disease that is particularly severe and difficult to treat.

riskFactors.factor: Close contact with enteroviral infection (day care, schools)
riskFactors.description: Enteroviruses spread through fecal-oral and respiratory routes in close-contact settings (day care, schools, households). Outbreaks of encephalitis reflect intense community transmission.
Children with asthma or reactive airway disease are disproportionately represented in EV-D68 neurological cases — EV-D68 respiratory infections are more severe in asthmatic children.

affectedDemographics: Children under 5 years bear the highest burden of enteroviral encephalitis
Children with asthma for EV-D68 AFM specifically
Immunocompromised patients (especially B-cell defects) for severe chronic enteroviral encephalitis
Adults in outbreak settings — EV-A71 can cause adult encephalitis in caregivers of affected children
diagnosisOverview: Enteroviral encephalitis requires a combination of clinical suspicion (summer-fall season, viral prodrome, CSF findings), neuroimaging, and laboratory confirmation. CSF analysis typically shows lymphocytic pleocytosis (50-500 cells/µL), normal glucose, and protein <150 mg/dL — much less inflammatory than bacterial meningitis. PCR of CSF for enterovirus is the diagnostic gold standard with sensitivity of 80-95% and specificity >95%. Multiplex PCR panels (FilmArray Meningitis/Encephalitis panel) include enterovirus and can return results in 1-3 hours. Throat swab PCR is more sensitive than CSF PCR because viral shedding from the throat is higher; stool PCR is positive for longer but is less specific for CNS disease. MRI brain shows hyperintense lesions in affected brain regions: brainstem in EV-A71 encephalitis, basal ganglia in some echovirus cases, and spinal cord gray matter in EV-D68 AFM. EEG shows generalized slowing or epileptiform activity. Blood PCR and serology (neutralizing antibody titers) are supportive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid molecular confirmation of enteroviral encephalitis. Part of FilmArray Meningitis/Encephalitis panel.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CSF sample in PCR panel. Results in 1-3 hours. Sensitivity 80-95% (higher if done within first 3 days of symptoms). Positive result confirms enteroviral encephalitis but cannot distinguish between enterovirus types.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Higher sensitivity than CSF PCR (throat viral load higher). Supports diagnosis when CSF PCR is negative.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Throat swab in viral transport medium. Results 1-3 days. Sensitivity 85-95% during acute infection. Also helps with epidemiological surveillance of circulating types.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain with contrast and diffusion-weighted imaging
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assess CNS involvement, characterize brain region involvement (brainstem, basal ganglia, cortex), and exclude other diagnoses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in brainstem (EV-A71), basal ganglia (echovirus), or normal in mild cases. EV-D68 AFM shows cervical cord T2 hyperintensities in anterior horn. No contrast enhancement in uncomplicated viral encephalitis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assess seizure activity (clinical and subclinical), evaluate encephalopathy severity, monitor treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Generalized slowing in encephalopathy, focal slowing in areas of inflammation, epileptiform discharges in seizure-prone patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Positive for longer (weeks after infection) than CSF or throat — useful for surveillance and epidemiological typing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Stool sample. Positive for enterovirus for 2-6 weeks after infection. Less specific for CNS disease — enterovirus may be present in stool from intestinal infection unrelated to encephalitis.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of enteroviral encephalitis is primarily supportive — no specific antiviral therapy is proven effective for non-polio enteroviruses. Management includes: antipyretics (paracetamol, ibuprofen) for fever; anticonvulsants (levetiracetam, valproic acid, or phenytoin) for seizures; osmotic therapy (mannitol, hypertonic saline) for raised intracranial pressure; ICU care with mechanical ventilation for respiratory compromise (brainstem involvement or status epilepticus). In immunocompromised patients with chronic enteroviral CNS disease (especially B-cell deficiencies), high-dose intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG 1-2 g/kg/month) or pleconaril (an enterovirus replication inhibitor, available through compassionate use) may be attempted. For EV-D68 AFM, no specific treatment exists — supportive care, intensive rehabilitation, and experimental approaches (fluoxetine, corticosteroids) have been tried with mixed results. For B-cell-deficient patients, IVIG (400-600 mg/kg every 2-4 weeks) with or without pleconaril offers the only established approach. Prevention: hand hygiene (soap and water, alcohol-based rub less effective against non-enveloped viruses), enteric precautions in hospitals, and for EV-A71, vaccine in China.

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive care and antipyretics
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of enteroviral encephalitis treatment. Maintaining hydration, controlling fever (paracetamol 10-15 mg/kg every 4-6 hours, max 75 mg/kg/day), and preventing secondary complications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Fever control improves comfort and reduces metabolic demand. Most enteroviral encephalitis cases resolve with supportive care alone.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: Paracetamol for fever, maintenance IV fluids, antiemetics, monitoring. Duration until clinical improvement and oral intake adequate.

medicalTreatments.name: Anticonvulsant therapy for seizures
medicalTreatments.description: Seizures in enteroviral encephalitis require anticonvulsant therapy. Levetiracetam is first-line due to minimal drug interactions and broad spectrum. Status epilepticus requires aggressive management with benzodiazepines followed by maintenance anticonvulsants.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Levetiracetam controls seizures in ~80% of viral encephalitis-related epilepsy. Status epilepticus requires aggressive treatment — mortality increases dramatically with prolonged seizures.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: Levetiracetam 20-30 mg/kg IV loading, then 20-30 mg/kg/day divided twice daily. May take 2-4 weeks before weaning considered. Many survivors require long-term anticonvulsants.

medicalTreatments.name: IVIG for chronic enteroviral CNS disease (B-cell deficiency)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose intravenous immunoglobulin provides passive neutralizing antibodies that help control enteroviral CNS disease in B-cell deficient patients. Monthly IVIG is standard of care for agammaglobulinemic patients with enteroviral encephalitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IVIG reduces enteroviral CNS disease frequency and severity in B-cell deficiency syndromes. Case series show 60-70% reduction in CNS events. Must be given regularly — breakthrough infections occur with missed doses.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: 400-600 mg/kg IV every 2-4 weeks (maintenance); 1-2 g/kg as loading dose. Trough IgG levels guide dosing. Lifelong in agammaglobulinemia.

medicalTreatments.name: Pleconaril (compassionate use for chronic enteroviral CNS disease)
medicalTreatments.description: An enterovirus capsid inhibitor that blocks viral attachment and entry into cells. Available through compassionate use protocol for chronic enteroviral CNS disease in immunocompromised patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case reports and small series suggest pleconaril may reduce enteroviral CNS viral load and clinical deterioration in chronic disease. Not FDA-approved; available only through expanded access programs.
medicalTreatments.typicalCourse: 5 mg/kg orally 3 times daily for 7-14 days (acute); 5 mg/kg 3 times daily for 14 days monthly (chronic suppressive). Monitor liver function.

surgicalOptions.name: Ventriculostomy for hydrocephalus
surgicalOptions.description: Enteroviral encephalitis can cause obstructive hydrocephalus from brain edema or (rarely) from particulate CSF blockage. External ventricular drain (EVD) is placed for acute hydrocephalus.
surgicalOptions.whatToExpect: Bedside or operating room procedure. EVD placed in frontal horn of lateral ventricle. Monitors ICP and allows CSF drainage. May be converted to VP shunt after acute phase.

alternativeTreatments.name: Corticosteroids for severe brain edema
alternativeTreatments.description: High-dose IV methylprednisolone has been tried in severe enteroviral encephalitis with cerebral edema, with anecdotal success. Not standard of care; rational is anti-inflammatory effect on cytokine-mediated brain swelling.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Limited evidence. Some clinicians use in severe cases with mass effect and herniation risk. Theoretical benefit of reducing inflammation-mediated damage.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: Methylprednisolone 10-30 mg/kg IV daily for 3-5 days with taper, or 1g IV daily for 3 days.

alternativeTreatments.name: Fluoxetine for EV-D68 AFM (investigational)
alternativeTreatments.description: Fluoxetine (SSRI) has in vitro activity against EV-D68 (blocks viral replication at concentrations achievable with standard doses). Retrospective data suggested benefit in AFM but prospective data are lacking.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Retrospective studies show possible faster recovery in AFM patients treated with fluoxetine 20-40 mg/day within 2 weeks of weakness onset. Not proven; clinical trial ongoing.
alternativeTreatments.typicalCourse: Fluoxetine 20 mg/day (adults), 10-20 mg/day (children) — standard antidepressant dose. Duration unclear — continued until plateau of recovery.

whySeeSpecialist: Enteroviral encephalitis requires neurology input for seizure management, ICU care, and assessment of neurological sequelae. Infectious disease specialists coordinate diagnostic workup, manage complex immunocompromised cases, and interact with public health for outbreak reporting. For EV-D68 AFM, neurology and rehabilitation specialists are essential.
doctorSelectionGuide: Go to a hospital with neurology, ICU capability, and CSF PCR (or access to reference lab). Suspected enteroviral encephalitis should be reported to public health authorities for surveillance. For AFM evaluation, seek a pediatric neurology center with MRI spine and EMG capability.
hospitalCriteria: Neurology service with experience in viral encephalitis management
ICU with mechanical ventilation capability for severe encephalitis and brainstem involvement
CSF PCR capability (multiplex meningitis/encephalitis panel or reference lab)
MRI brain and spine capability for AFM evaluation
Rehabilitation service for AFM and post-encephalitis recovery
keyFacilities: ICU with ICP monitoring capability for severe encephalitis
Neurology with EEG monitoring for refractory seizures
MRI with spinal cord imaging for AFM workup
Rehabilitation with pediatric PT/OT for AFM weakness recovery
Public health notification system for enteroviral outbreaks

costBreakdown.consultation: Neurology consultation: ₹500-₹2,000. ICU care: ₹5,000-₹20,000/day. Infectious disease consultation: ₹500-₹1,500.
costBreakdown.diagnosticTests: CSF PCR (multiplex panel): ₹3,000-₹8,000. MRI brain: ₹3,000-₹8,000. MRI spine (for AFM): ₹4,000-₹10,000. EEG: ₹800-₹2,500.

insuranceGuide: Enteroviral encephalitis and AFM are covered as serious neurological infections. In India, Ayushman Bharat PMJAY covers hospitalization for encephalitis. Chronic conditions like post-encephalitic epilepsy or AFM weakness require long-term coverage.
financialAssistance: IVIG and pleconaril are expensive. Patient assistance programs exist for pleconaril through manufacturer. IVIG may be partially covered under national health programs for immunodeficiency syndromes.
preventionStrategies: Hand hygiene with soap and water — most effective prevention; alcohol-based rub less effective against non-enveloped enteroviruses
Avoid close contact with people with enteroviral illness during summer-fall season
Disinfect surfaces in daycare/school settings during outbreaks
Polio vaccination (IPV) — prevents poliomyelitis; maintained immunization essential
EV-A71 vaccine (available in China since 2016) — prevents severe EV-A71 disease including brainstem encephalitis
Prompt reporting of clusters of neurological illness to public health authorities
lifestyleModifications: Teach children thorough hand washing with soap and water after toilet use and before eating
Avoid sharing utensils, cups, and towels with sick individuals
Keep children home from school during acute enteroviral illness to prevent spread
For children with asthma, maintain good asthma control — reduces EV-D68 severe respiratory disease risk
Ensure polio vaccination schedule is complete — critical for poliomyelitis prevention

recommended: Soft, easily swallowable foods during recovery if pharyngeal weakness is present
Nutrient-dense diet for recovery — protein, vitamins B and D, omega-3 fatty acids for neural recovery
Adequate hydration — fever increases fluid requirements
Small frequent meals if fatigue limits intake
avoid: Large meals if consciousness is impaired — risk of aspiration
Spicy or very hot foods when oral sensitivity is altered
Raw undercooked foods during enteroviral illness outbreaks

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is not appropriate during active encephalitis with fever and neurological symptoms. After recovery, rehabilitation exercises are critical for AFM patients — intensive PT/OT can improve functional outcomes substantially even when residual weakness persists. Gradual return to activities as tolerated. No specific exercise prevents enteroviral infection — hand hygiene is the only proven prevention.
dailyManagement: Monitor for seizure recurrence — if new seizures occur, seek emergency care
In children with asthma, continue asthma medications and seek care early for respiratory symptoms (EV-D68 triggers asthma exacerbations)
If limb weakness or facial droop develops, seek emergency care immediately — may indicate AFM or stroke
Report any outbreak of neurological illness to local health authority
For chronic enteroviral CNS disease in immunodeficient patients, maintain regular IVIG schedule — missing doses risks breakthrough infection
Seizure precautions during recovery — no swimming without supervision, avoid heights, bathroom rails until medically cleared
prognosis: Most enteroviral encephalitis cases (echovirus, coxsackie) have favorable outcomes — 70-85% recover fully with supportive care within 2-4 weeks. Some patients have persistent headaches, mild cognitive changes, or new-onset epilepsy. EV-A71 brainstem encephalitis has worse prognosis — 5-10% fatality rate, 15-20% with neurological sequelae (cognitive impairment, motor deficits, ataxia). EV-D68 AFM has the most severe outcomes — approximately 10-15% mortality, and among survivors, most have permanent residual weakness despite intensive rehabilitation. Poliomyelitis has the worst prognosis — permanent flaccid paralysis affecting limb, respiratory, or bulbar muscles.
recoveryTimeline: Mild enteroviral encephalitis: resolution of fever within 3-5 days, improvement in mental status over 1-2 weeks, full recovery in 2-4 weeks. Severe cases with prolonged altered consciousness: recovery over 4-12 weeks with rehabilitation. EEG normalization may lag behind clinical recovery. New-onset seizures may persist for months to years and require long-term anticonvulsant therapy. AFM recovery is measured in months to years — some improvement continues for 2+ years with intensive rehabilitation.
complications: Post-encephalitic epilepsy — new-onset seizures in 10-20% of survivors; may be permanent
Cognitive impairment — memory, attention, and executive function deficits particularly in children; may affect school performance
Acute flaccid myelitis (AFM) — permanent flaccid limb weakness from anterior horn cell destruction; most severe in EV-D68 infection
Brainstem dysfunction — swallowing difficulty, breathing irregularities, cranial nerve palsies; most severe in EV-A71
Hydrocephalus — obstructive hydrocephalus from brain edema; may require VP shunt

supportResources.name: CDC Enterovirus Information
supportResources.description: Up-to-date surveillance data, outbreak alerts, and clinical guidance for enteroviral disease including EV-D68 and EV-A71

supportResources.name: WHO EV-A71 Fact Sheet 2023
supportResources.description: Global EV-A71 epidemiology, outbreak guidance, and vaccine information

supportResources.name: MPN Foundation - AFM Information
supportResources.description: Patient resources and family support for those affected by acute flaccid myelitis

confusedWithConditions.name: HSV encephalitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause encephalitis in children. HSV has temporal lobe predilection (MRI T2 hyperintensity), more rapid deterioration, and requires acyclovir treatment urgently. Enterovirus tends to have more brainstem involvement, less focal temporal lobe findings. Distinction requires CSF PCR for both.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial meningitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bacterial meningitis has more acute onset, very high CSF WBC (>1,000 neutrophils), very low glucose, and bacterial growth on culture/Gram stain. Enteroviral encephalitis has lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, and negative bacterial studies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ADEM follows viral infection or vaccination by 1-3 weeks and shows large, confluent white matter lesions on MRI. Enteroviral encephalitis involves gray matter and brainstem more prominently. Distinction by CSF PCR and MRI pattern.

coOccurringConditions.name: Hand-foot-mouth disease
coOccurringConditions.reason: EV-A71 and coxsackie A viruses cause the characteristic vesicular rash on hands, feet, and oral mucosa; its presence in a child with encephalitis strongly suggests enteroviral etiology

coOccurringConditions.name: Acute flaccid myelitis
coOccurringConditions.reason: EV-D68 (and rarely other enteroviruses) cause AFM — flaccid limb weakness with spinal cord gray matter lesions. Same viral infection affects brain and spinal cord.

coOccurringConditions.name: Myocarditis
coOccurringConditions.reason: Coxsackie B viruses and EV-A71 can cause viral myocarditis alongside encephalitis — chest pain, elevated troponins, ECG changes, heart failure in severe cases

relatedConditions.name: Aseptic (viral) meningitis
relatedConditions.relationship: Enteroviruses are the most common cause of viral meningitis; encephalitis represents more severe CNS invasion than meningitis

relatedConditions.name: Acute flaccid myelitis
relatedConditions.relationship: EV-D68-associated neurological syndrome with flaccid paralysis; often co-occurs with or follows respiratory enterovirus infection

relatedConditions.name: HSV encephalitis
relatedConditions.relationship: Most common sporadic viral encephalitis; requires urgent acyclovir treatment — distinguished from enteroviral by MRI and CSF PCR

faqs.question: What is enteroviral encephalitis?
faqs.answer: Enteroviral encephalitis is brain inflammation caused by enteroviruses — common viruses that usually cause mild illness (cold, fever, hand-foot-mouth disease) but occasionally spread to the brain. It's most common in children under 5, especially during summer and early fall when enteroviruses circulate widely.

faqs.question: How does a virus get into the brain?
faqs.answer: Enteroviruses first infect the throat and gut lining. From there, they enter the bloodstream (viremia) and cross the blood-brain barrier — a protective wall around the brain and spinal cord. The virus then infects brain cells, causing inflammation that disrupts their function.

faqs.question: What is the difference between meningitis and encephalitis?
faqs.answer: Meningitis is infection/inflammation of the meninges — the protective membranes covering the brain and spinal cord. Encephalitis is infection/inflammation of the brain tissue itself. Meningitis causes headache, neck stiffness, and fever. Encephalitis adds altered mental status, confusion, personality changes, seizures, and focal neurological deficits like weakness or vision problems.

faqs.question: How is enteroviral encephalitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The doctor will take a sample of spinal fluid (lumbar puncture) to look for signs of infection — enteroviruses cause a lymphocytic pleocytosis (elevated white cells), normal sugar, and mildly elevated protein. A PCR test on the spinal fluid can identify enterovirus RNA directly. An MRI of the brain may show areas of inflammation. Blood tests, throat swabs, and stool tests also help.

faqs.question: Is there a specific treatment for enteroviral encephalitis?
faqs.answer: No proven antiviral treatment exists for non-polio enteroviruses. Treatment is supportive: medicines to control fever, drugs to stop seizures, and ICU care if the patient is very ill. In rare cases of chronic enteroviral CNS disease in patients with weak immune systems, intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) or a drug called pleconaril may be used.

faqs.question: What is EV-D68 and why is it causing acute flaccid myelitis (AFM)?
faqs.answer: EV-D68 is an enterovirus that primarily causes respiratory illness (cold, wheezing, asthma exacerbations) but has an unusual ability to also infect motor neurons — the nerve cells that control limb movement. When it does this, it causes AFM — sudden-onset weakness in one or more limbs. AFM has been reported in outbreaks in the US, Europe, and Asia since 2014, primarily in children.

faqs.question: Can enteroviral encephalitis be prevented?
faqs.answer: There's no vaccine for most enteroviruses (only for poliovirus and EV-A71 in China). Prevention focuses on reducing transmission: wash hands with soap and water (most effective), avoid close contact with sick people, disinfect surfaces, and keep children home from school during acute illness. The EV-A71 vaccine (available in China) prevents severe EV-A71 brain disease.

faqs.question: Is enteroviral encephalitis contagious?
faqs.answer: The enteroviruses that cause encephalitis are contagious — they spread through respiratory droplets (coughing, sneezing), fecal-oral route (contaminated hands, surfaces), and sometimes from infected people who have no symptoms. However, encephalitis itself is not contagious — only the enterovirus infection that caused it is.

faqs.question: What is the outcome for children who get enteroviral encephalitis?
faqs.answer: Most children recover fully within 2-4 weeks with supportive care. However, some have lasting effects: new seizures requiring anticonvulsants, mild cognitive or memory problems, or persistent headaches. In severe cases (EV-A71 brainstem encephalitis or EV-D68 AFM), outcomes can be serious — permanent limb weakness, breathing difficulties, or in rare cases, death.

faqs.question: What is hand-foot-mouth disease and does it always lead to encephalitis?
faqs.answer: Hand-foot-mouth disease (HFMD) is a common childhood illness caused by enteroviruses (especially EV-A71 and coxsackie A). It causes fever, mouth sores, and a rash on hands, feet, and buttocks. Most children recover in 7-10 days without problems. Only a tiny fraction (less than 0.1%) develop neurological complications including encephalitis — usually when a particularly neurovirulent strain (like EV-A71) is circulating.

faqs.question: My child had a seizure with fever — could it be enteroviral encephalitis?
faqs.answer: Febrile seizures are common in young children (6 months to 5 years) and usually are simple (last less than 15 minutes, one side of the body, occur once in 24 hours) with full recovery. Encephalitis seizures tend to be more severe (prolonged, repeated, focal), accompanied by prolonged altered mental status, and other neurological signs. If your child has these features, seek urgent medical evaluation with lumbar puncture and MRI.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from enteroviral encephalitis take?
faqs.answer: Mild cases recover in 1-2 weeks. More severe cases with prolonged altered consciousness, seizures, or focal deficits require 4-12 weeks for initial recovery, with further improvement over 6-12 months through rehabilitation. Children with persistent deficits may need years of physical/occupational therapy. EEG abnormalities may persist longer than clinical symptoms.

metaTitle: Enteroviral Encephalitis: EV-D68, AFM, Symptoms & Treatment | AiHealz
metaDescription: Complete guide to enteroviral encephalitis: EV-D68 acute flaccid myelitis, EV-A71 brainstem encephalitis, hand-foot-mouth complications, diagnosis and recovery.

images.caption: Hand-foot-mouth disease lesions — EV-A71 and coxsackie A viruses cause this characteristic rash, which may precede neurological complications.
